questions.question: An upset pancake forging is examined for inherited stringers and bursts. Because grain flow turns radially outward, those discontinuities lie mostly parallel to the end faces. The primary scan should be:
options: A radial straight beam from the rim
An axial straight beam from a flat end face
A 70-degree shear wave from the rim
A surface wave around the circumference
questions.explanation: In an upset disk the flow — and everything elongated along it — lies flat, parallel to the faces; an axial beam from the face meets those reflectors broadside, while a rim scan sees only their edges.

questions.question: A forging lap is difficult for a perpendicular straight beam to detect because it:
options: Sits too deep at the centerline to reach
Is a tight, oxide-filled fold lying at a shallow angle to the surface, presenting almost no reflecting area to the beam
Only occurs in nonferrous forgings
Reflects sound only above 10 MHz
questions.explanation: Laps are folded-over surface metal, nearly parallel to the surface and very tight; angle beams, surface waves, or magnetic particle testing are the practical detection routes.

questions.question: A 10 mm diameter, 5 MHz straight-beam probe is used on a steel forging (5,920 m/s). Its near-field length N = D²f/4v is about:
options: 5 mm
11 mm
21 mm
42 mm
questions.explanation: N = (0.010 m)² × 5,000,000 Hz ÷ (4 × 5,920 m/s) ≈ 0.021 m ≈ 21 mm; amplitude oscillates inside the near field, so evaluate against a DAC or beyond N.

questions.question: A coarse-grained casting fills the screen with grass at 5 MHz. The underlying physics is that Rayleigh-regime grain scattering scales roughly with:
options: Frequency
Frequency squared
Frequency to the fourth power
The inverse of frequency
questions.explanation: Scattering rises about as f⁴ when the wavelength is much larger than the grains — halving frequency cuts grain noise roughly sixteen-fold, which is why castings are tested at 1–2.25 MHz or lower.

questions.question: Two casting zones show different screens: zone A has many small, separate echoes with a modest back-echo drop; zone B has a ragged, traveling echo cluster with the back echo nearly gone. The most likely pairing is:
options: A: gas porosity; B: shrinkage
A: shrinkage; B: gas porosity
A: cold shuts; B: hot tears
A: unfused chaplets; B: sand inclusions
questions.explanation: Rounded, discrete gas pores return many small echoes while passing much of the beam; ragged shrinkage in last-to-freeze metal both reflects and blocks, producing shifting clusters and severe back-reflection loss.

questions.question: To keep signal-to-noise workable, a casting is examined at 1 MHz with a 25 mm diameter element. The beam-spread half-angle, from sin γ = 1.22 v/(D f), is about:
options: 8 degrees
17 degrees
29 degrees
45 degrees
questions.explanation: sin γ = 1.22 × 5,920 ÷ (0.025 × 1,000,000) ≈ 0.289, so γ ≈ 17° — the large element keeps the low-frequency beam usefully narrow, which is why casting probes are big.

questions.question: On a through-transmission C-scan of a solid laminate, a delamination and a porosity field typically appear, respectively, as:
options: A sharp-edged zone of near-total transmission loss, and a mottled region of diffuse partial loss
A mottled diffuse region, and a sharp-edged dead zone
Two identical bright spots of increased transmission
Neither appears, since TTU cannot detect planar defects
questions.explanation: A delamination blocks transmission almost completely inside a crisp boundary, while distributed micro-voids scatter the beam partially and unevenly, painting a mottled gray loss across the affected region.

questions.question: Honeycomb sandwich panels are routinely examined by through-transmission rather than pulse-echo because:
options: Through-transmission resolves each cell wall of the core
The core scatters and absorbs a pulse-echo beam, while through-transmission only asks whether energy crossed — at the cost of depth information
Pulse-echo cannot couple to composite skins
Through-transmission requires no fixture alignment
questions.explanation: A pulse-echo beam dies in the core, but a transmitter-receiver pair straddling the panel still measures transmitted energy; defects appear as transmission loss with no depth data, which is acceptable for skin-to-core disbond mapping.

questions.question: At a steel-to-rubber bonded interface (Z about 46.5 and 1.9 MRayl), the fraction of sound energy reflected back into the steel is roughly:
questions.explanation: R = ((46.5 − 1.9)/(46.5 + 1.9))² ≈ 0.85. The ~15% leaking into the rubber at every bounce is what makes a bonded lining's echo train decay fast, while a disbond (steel-air, ~100% reflection) rings on.

questions.question: A surface-wave sweep is chosen to find shallow cracks in a glass edge. Rayleigh waves suit this job because they:
options: Concentrate their energy at the surface, sample about one wavelength deep, and follow gentle contours
Travel faster than longitudinal waves in glass
Propagate only in metals
Are unaffected by anything touching the surface
questions.explanation: Rayleigh waves hug the surface to a depth of roughly one wavelength and follow gentle curvature, making them highly sensitive to the shallow surface cracks that govern fracture in brittle glass.
